title: Mobile Power System FZE v Dioubate and Others (ECW/CCJ/APP/19/18/TP; ECW/CCJ/JUD/05/26) [2026] ECOWASCJ 3 (30 January 2026)
text:
.• 
IN THE COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE ECONOMIC COMMUNITY OF WEST 
AFRICAN STATES 
In the Matter of 
MOBILE POWER SYSTEMS FZE 
v. 
IBRAHIMA KASSUS DIOUBATE, K. ENERGIES.A. AND STATE OF 
GUINEA 
Application No. ECW/CCJ/APP/19/18/TP - Judgment No. ECW/CCJ/JUD/05/26 
JUDGMENT 
ABUJA 
On the 30th of January 2026 
1 


APPLICATION No: ECW/CCJ/APP/19/18/TP 
JUDGMENT NO. ECW /CCJ/JUD/05/26 
MOBILE POWER SYSTEMS FZE 
third-party OPPONENT 
v. 
IBRAHIMA KASSUS DIOUBATE, 
K. ENERGIES.A. AND STATE OF RESPONDENTS IN THIRD-PARTY 
GUINEA 
PROCEEDINGS 
COMPOSITION OF THE COURT PANEL 
Hon. 
Justice 
Ricardo 
Claudio 
Monteiro 
GON(;ALVES-
Hon. Justice Dupe ATOKI-
Hon. Justice Gberi-Be OUATTARA-
ASSISTED BY: 
Mr. Gaye SOWE 
REPRESENTATION OF THE PARTIES: 
-
Presiding/Judge-
Rapporteur 
-
Member 
-
Member 
- Registrar 
K. T. Turaki SAN, FCIArb, 
Rasky Gbinigie Esq, 
- For Mobile Power Systems FZE 
Adeyemi Shekoni-Larval Esq., 
Nasir Saidu Esq, 
A. 1. Muhammed Esq, 
Mukhtar U. Bunza Esq 
Me So Abdourahmane, 
Me Bernard Feindouno, 
Me Bathily Demba Cire 
Me So Mamadou Papa Samba 
2 
- For Ibrahima Kassus Dioubate and 
K. Energie S.A. 


Me Joachim GBILIMOU 
I. 
JUDGMENT 
- For the State of Guinea 
1. This is the judgment of the Court read in a virtual public hearing, in accordance with 
Article 8 (1) of the Practice Directions on Electronic Case Management and Virtual 
Court Sessions, 2020. 
II. 
DESCRIPTION OF THE PARTIES 
2. The Applicant is Mobile Power Systems - FZE, assisted by K. T. Turaki SAN, 
FCIArb, Rasky Gbinigie Esq., Adeyemi Shekoni-Larval Esq., Nasir Saidu Esq., A. 
1. Muhammed Esq. e MukhtarU. BunzaEsq. 
3. Mobile Power Systems - FZE is a company registered and incorporated in Dubai, 
United Arab Emirates, under the Dubai Silicon Oasis Authority (DOSA), 100% 
owned by Alexandre Sam Zormati, with registered offices in: Dso - Hq - 17 - 703. 
Dubai Silicon Oasis. Dubai United Arab Emirates. 
4. The Respondents are, on the one hand, Ibrahim Kassus Dioubate and K. Energie 
S.A., and the State of Guinea. 
5. Mr. Ibrahim Kassus Dioubate is a Guinean economic operator, shareholder and 
general manager of K. Energie S.A., with domicile in Kipe, Commune de Ratoma 2, 
Conakry, State of Guinea. 
6. K. Energie S.A. was registered in the Registre du Commerce et du Credit Mobilier 
( Commercial and Mobile Credit Register - RCCM) on 22nd May 2014 under number 
GC-KAL/052.782/2014; its shares are held 93% by Miami Capital Holding 
Corporation, 5% by Miami Group Inc., 1 % by Mr Kassus Ibrahima Dioubate and 1 % 
3 


by Mr Sam Alexandre Zonnati. Its registered office is in the Coronthie district, 
Municipality of Kaloum. 
III. 
INTRODUCTION 
7. On July 9, 2020, the Court delivered Judgment ECW/CCJ/JUD/14/2020 in case 
ECW/CCJ/APP/19/18, in which Mr. Ibrahima Kassus Dioubate and K. Energie S.A. 
are the Applicants and the State of Guinea is the Respondent. 
8. In that judgment, the Court declared itself competent and ruled admissible the claim 
of Mr. Ibrahima Kassus Dioubate and K. Energie S.A., for violation of human rights. 
9. It also declared that the State of Guinea had violated K. Energie S.A.'s right to 
property, protected and guaranteed by Articles 14 of the ACHPR and I 7 of the 
UDHR. The right in question relates to a group of assets: power stations with mobile 
turbines, associated facilities, namely transformers and other equipment necessary 
for the production and distribution of electricity; buildings and real estate constructed 
to house offices and residences for employees. On the other hand, it declared that the 
State of Guinea had not violated the applicants' right to have their case heard or their 
right to equal protection of the law. 
IO.It ordered the Guinean State to return to K Energie SA all its assets, of which it had 
been dispossessed, consisting of power stations with mobile turbines, related 
facilities, namely transformers and other equipment necessary for the production and 
distribution of electricity; buildings and real estate constructed to house offices and 
residences for employees. The Court also ordered the Guinean State to pay K Energie 
S.A. 47,500,000 (forty-seven million, five hundred thousand) US dollars, as 
compensation for having been deprived of the enjoyment of its property. 
11. The company Mobile Power Systems - FZE, which did not participate in the main 
proceedings, filed, under the terms of Article 91 of its Rules of Procedure, a third-
4 
r)) t~ 


party 
proceedings 
application 
for 
the 
annulment 
of Judgment 
No. 
ECW/CCJ/JUD/14/2020 of July 9, 2020, in the Case ECW/CCJ/APP/19/18, Mr. 
lbrahima Kassus Dioubate and K. Energie S.A. v. the Republic of Guinea. 
IV. 
PROCEEDINGS BEFORE THE COURT 
12.The Applicant, Mobile Power Systems FZE, submitted a third-party proceedings 
application at the same time as an application for expedited procedure, all dated 
September 8, 2020, received at the Court Registry on September 18, 2020. 
13.The pleadings in response to the the third-party proceedings and the application for 
expedited procedure from Kassus Dioubate and K Energie SA, both dated November 
4, 2020, were received at the Court Registry on November 13, 2020. 
14. The State of Guinea's reply to the third-party proceedings, dated October 22, 2020, 
was received at the Court Registry on December 2, 2020. 
15. The third-party opponent Mobile Power Systems FZE, filed an application for 
judgment by default on April 19, 2021, which was received at the Court Registry on 
April 22, 2021. 
16. On May 17, 2021, Kassus Dioubate and K Energie SA filed their reply to the 
application for judgment by default, which was received at the Court Registry on 
May 18, 2021. 
17. The Court heard the parties at the court hearing on May 18, 2023. The State of Guinea 
was not present at this hearing, at which the third-party opponent withdrew its 
application for an expedited procedure and its application for a default judgment. 
However, the Court decided that only Mobile Power Systems - FZE and Ibrahim 
Kassus Dioubate and K. Energie SA would be heard. The parties presented their 
submissions to the Court. 
5 


V. 
THE CASE OF THE APPLICANT/THIRD PATY OPPONENT 
a. 
Summary of Facts 
18.Mobile Power Systems - FZE declares that it is a company incorporated and 
registered in Dubai: United Arab Emirates, in accordance with the laws, rules and 
regulations of the Dubai Silicon Oasis Authority (DOSA). It attached a copy of its 
commercial license. It states that its activities include electricity generation, 
transmission and distribution of equipment at local and international level. The 
company is 100% owned by Sam Alexandre Zormati. The latter and Ibrahim Kassus 
Dioubate are the promoters of the company K Energie S.A., registered in Guinea and 
the company Miami Capital Holding Corporation ( or Miami Capital Group Holding), 
created on May 22, 2014 under number 2014/IBCl 7960, having Ibrahima Kassus 
Dioubate and Alexandre Sam Zormati as the main shareholders, each with 49% of 
the capital. 
19. The third-party Opponent alleges that Miami Capital Group Corporation purchased 
three (03) Pac mobile turbine units, as well as related electric generators and auxiliary 
parts from PW Power Systems Inc., the owner of the turbines; that, under an 
equipment lease agreement dated 5 January 2015, Miami Capital Group Corporation 
leased the aforementioned mobile turbines to K Energie S.A. and Miami Capital 
Holding Corporation to enable them to perform a power purchase agreement with 
the State of Guinea ( a copy of the aforementioned agreement is attached to the 
application); that of the three mobile Pac turbines, one was subsequently returned, 
leaving the remaining two to K Energie S.A. and Miami Capital Holding 
Corporation; that the two hydroelectric power plants and related facilities were then 
installed on the land made available to K Energie S.A. by the State of Guinea; that 
after development and installation, K Energie S.A. began selling its production to the 
State of Guinea in accordance with the terms of their contract; that, however, the 
6 
fb 


contract was tenninated mid-tenn because the State of Guinea failed to fulfill its 
payment obligation under the contract; that the said termination frustrated the lease 
agreement that K Energie S.A. and Miami Capital Holding Corporation had signed 
with Miami Capital Group Corporation. 
20. The third-party Opponent maintains that, as a result of this fact, Miami Capital Group 
Corporation regained possession of the leased equipment, which, in any case, always 
remained the exclusive property of the lessor, namely Miami Capital Group 
Corporation; that, after the tennination of the power purchase agreement and the 
lease agreement, Miami Capital Group Corporation, in its capacity as owner of the 
two Mobile Pac turbines, sold them to the Applicant, Mobile Power Systems - FZE 
(the third-party Opponent attached two documents in support of this statement, both 
entitled 'transfer title', both dated 7 December 2016, which allegedly certify the 
transfer of ownership of the turbines with serial numbers 1403 and 1404 from Miami 
Capital Group Corporation to Mobile Power Systems - FZE); that the latter company 
consequently took possession of the power stations and associated facilities as the 
new owners and retained its infrastructure, personnel and management; that, in order 
to continue to meet the electricity needs of the State of Guinea, the third-party 
Opponent signed a wholesale power purchase agreement with GOE Sarlu, another 
local company, which in turn will sell to the State of Guinea. 
21.Mobile Power Systems - FZE asserts that K Energie S.A. has never owned the 
mobile turbine power stations and associated facilities; that Ibrahim Kassus Dioubate 
is fully aware of the fact that ownership of the power station has not been transferred 
to K Energie S.A. by neither Miami Capital Group Corporation nor Mobile Power 
Systems - FZE; that K. Energie S.A. and Kassus Dioubate fraudulently concealed 
the identity of Mobile Power Systems - FZE, which is the true owner of the power 
plants, and claimed ownership of them, with the intention of fraudulently distorting 
7 


the facts and misleading the Court of Justice in order to obtain an advantage, which, 
incidentally, they obtained, by virtue of the fact that the Court of Justice delivered a 
judgment in favor of K Energie S.A. on 9 July 2020. 
b. 
Pleas in Law 
22. The Applicant relied its claims on the following: 
1. 
Article 91 of the Rules of Procedure of the Court of Justice of the 
Economic Community of West African States (ECOW AS), 2002. 
11. 
Article 10 of Supplementary Protocol A/SP .1/01/05 amending Protocol 
A/Pl/7 /91 on the Community Court of Justice; 
c. 
Reliefs Sought 
23. The Applicant therefore seeks from the Community Court of Justice the following 
reliefs: 
1. 
Declare that K Energie and Ibrahim Kassus Oioubate fraudulently falsified 
the facts invoked in their application and thus misled the Court into ruling 
in their favor on July 9, 2020. 
u. 
Consequently, the Court of Justice should graciously issue an order 
annulling its Judgment No. ECW/CCJ/JUD/14/2020 of9 July 2020 in the 
case No. ECW/CCJ/APP/50/18. 
VI. 
THE CASE OF THE RESPONDENTS IN THE THIRD-PARTY 
PROCEEDINGS 
a. 
Summary of Facts 
8 


24. The claims of Mobile Power Systems - FZE are refuted by K. Energie S.A., 
which disputes the legal existence of the company and claims that the evidence 
it presents is "false". 
25.K Energie S.A. adds that, through third-party proceedings, Mobile Power 
Systems - FZE intends to challenge a judgment delivered against it by the 
Guinean courts, namely Judgment No. 129 of November 22, 2017, of the 
Criminal Division of the Conakry Court of Appeal, which convicted Sam 
Alexandre Zormati for misuse of social assets, on the grounds that he 
misappropriated K. Energie S.A. power plants and favored a company in 
which he had a direct interest, namely Miami Capital Group Corporation, of 
which he is the sole shareholder. It alleges that, aware that any claim by the 
latter company would hit a brick wall, Alexandre Zormati sought to use 
another of his companies, Mobil Power Systems - FZE, of which he was also 
the sole shareholder, to claim ownership of the power plants, after having 
arranged a transfer between the two, which is in fact fictitious; that it is this 
context that makes it possible to understand the intervention of Mobil Power 
Systems - FZE, which was deliberately concealed from the proceedings by the 
parties to the initial proceedings in order to mislead the Court; that this 
assertion is fanciful, insofar as the allegations concerning the ownership of 
Mobil Power System and its existence were presented by the State of Guinea 
in the main proceedings, with the production of documents signed by Sam 
Alexandre Zormati, and were correctly rejected by the Court. K Energie S.A. 
refers to paragraphs 285 and 296 to 301 of the judgment. 
26.K Energie S.A. maintains that the equipment lease agreement dated January 
5, 2015, signed between Miami Capital Group Corporation, on the one hand, 
and Miami Capital Holding Corporation and K Energie S.A., on the other, is 
9 


a forgery because the commercial registration number of Miami Capital Group 
Corporation, i.e., #18967#, had not yet been assigned to it on the date the 
agreement was signed; the company was only registered on January 22, 2016, 
under No. 2016/IBC18967 and could not, therefore, in 2015, acquire power 
plants and sign a document with its commercial registration number. 
27 .K Energie S.A. points out that the purchase agreement for the power plants, 
dated January 12, 2015, bears the signature of PW Power Systems Inc. and 
Miami Capital Group Holding Corporation, a company created on May 22, 
2014 under number 2014/IBCl 7960, having Ibrahima Kassus Dioubate and 
Alexandre Sam Zormati as the main shareholders, each with 49% of the 
capital. The contract for the purchase of the power plants had been signed by 
Alexandre Sam Zormati. It added that it was subsequently found that the 
purchase agreement could not be used for the transaction with UBA bank; that, 
for this reason, K Energie S.A., which was already a customer of the bank, 
received a formal offer of sale, which K Energie S.A. accepted by ordering the 
issuance of letters of credit for US$17 million and US$34 million for the 
acquisition of three power plants and payments of US$4,250,000 per month; 
at the time of delivery, the procedure and management manuals issued by PW 
Power Systems Inc. are marked "Customer: K Energie". 
28.For all these reasons, K Energie S.A. concludes that the lease agreement is a 
forgery. 
29. With regard to the articles of association and title deeds of Mobil Power 
Systems - FZE, K Energie S.A. states that, based on an analysis of commercial 
license no. 3250, issued on October 18, 2018, and valid until October 20, 2020, 
and the two title deeds of transfer no. 1 and no. 2, dated December 7, 2016, it 
appears that Mobile Power Systems - FZE transferred the assets in question to 
10 


the company with registration no. 17204 and that, in addition, the said 
company has two different registration numbers: no. 3250 and no. 17204; that, 
in reality, these are two separate legal entities under the same company name, 
one (No. 3250) wholly owned by Sam Zormati and the other (No. 17204) 
owned by Sam Zormati and his wife; that, since the company with registration 
number 17204 acquired the power plants, the company with registration 
number 3250 cannot bring the matter before the Court; that, even if it were 
accepted that it was the same company, the inconsistency of the documents, 
particularly with regard to the dates of transfer of ownership and the signing 
of the transfer documents, leads to the conclusion that it was a fictitious 
transfer of ownership orchestrated by Sam Zormati to protect the assets from 
possible claims, since he knew he was being sued for misappropriating the 
assets ofK Energie S.A. in the benefit of Miami Capital Group Corporation; 
that further evidence is provided by the fact that Mobil Power Systems - FZE 
with number 17204 was registered on November 3, 2017, i.e., nineteen days 
before the date of the conviction handed down by the Criminal Division of the 
Conakry Court of Appeal; that it is clear that Sam Zormati is attempting to 
prevent the power plants from being returned to the assets ofK Energie S.A. 
30.K Energie S.A. recalls that, given the relative effect of judicial decisions, it 
submitted an application to the ordinary courts of Guinea for the restitution of 
the power plants under the pretext of property assessment; that it sued Sam 
Zormati, Mobil Power Systems - FZE, Miami Capital Group Corporation, 
EDG Sarlu, and the State of Guinea; that the proceedings have not yet been 
co:o.cluded, but that a first judgment delivered on September 19, 2019, under 
No. 148 by the Conakry Court of First Instance "ordered the restitution of the 
aforementioned power plants and related facilities owned by the Guinean 
electricity company (GDE) Sarlu to K Energie ". 
11 


31.Finally, it states that no evidence of ownership by Miami Capital Group 
Corporation has been produced; that it could not transfer more rights than it 
possesses; and that Mobile Power Systems - FZE does not justify when or how it 
would have recovered the power plants and associated facilities. 
b. 
Pleas in Law 
32.The Respondents do not invoke any pleas in law. 
c. 
Reliefs Sought 
33.The Respondents therefore seek from the Community Court of Justice the 
following reliefs: 
1. 
To find that the ownership claimed by MOBIL POWER SYSTEMS is not 
proven, contrary to that ofK ENERGIE SA; 
u. 
To 
dismiss 
the 
application 
for 
annulment 
of 
Judgment 
ECW/CCJ/JUD/14/2020 of July 9, 2020 made by MOBIL POWER 
SYSTEMS FZE; 
m. 
To uphold, therefore, JudgmentECW/CCJ/JUD/14/2020 of July 9, 2020 in all 
its provisions; 
1v. 
To order MOBIL POWER SYSTEMS FZE to pay K ENERGIE SA and 
Ibrahima Kassus DIOUBA TE the sum of one hundred million CF A francs as 
compensation for the economic, material and non-material damage suffered; 
v. 
To order MOBIL POWER SYSTEMS FZE to pay all the costs. 
VII. 
THE CASE OF THE STATE OF GUINEA 
34. The Guinean State supports the opposition of third parties and maintains that the 
documentary evidence produced unequivocally supports the annulment of the 
contested judgment. In fact, the memorandum dated September 9, 2020, issued by 
the lawyer of the company GDE-SARL and addressed to the Minister of Justice, 
12 


Keeper of the Seals, to which a set of supporting documents is attached, shows 
that the company K-Energie is not, and never has been, the owner of the mobile 
turbine power plants or the related facilities, namely transformers and other 
equipment essential for the production and distribution of electricity. 
35.Such documents include, in particular, the final order, subsequent to the provisional 
order of the United States District Court for the District of Maryland, whereby the 
aforementioned turbines and related accessories were recognized as the property of 
Miami Capital Group Corporation, and in which K-Energie and Ibrahima Kassus 
Dioubate were also ordered to refrain from any interference with the possession or 
ownership rights of that company over those assets. The lease agreement under 
which such assets had been leased to K-Energie and Miami Capital Holding 
Corporation is also included. 
b. 
Pleas in Law 
36.The State of Guinea does not invoke any pleas in law. 
c. 
Reliefs Sought 
37.The State of Guinea prays the Court to: 
1. 
Declare this action by Societe Mobile Power Systems F-ZE admissible and 
well founded; 
11. 
Strike out the Judgment No. ECW/CCJ/JUG/14/2020 rendered by the 
ECOW AS Court of Justice; 
111. 
Acquit the State of Guinea of the condemnations unduly imposed on it by 
the aforementioned Judgment; 
1v. 
Order, by way of counterclaim, K. ENERGIE and Mr. lbrahima Kassus 
DIOUBATE to pay the Guinean State the sum of 1,500,000,000 FCF A as 
compensation; 
v. 
Dismiss all their claims, arguments and allegations in opposition; 
13 


v1. 
Order them to pay all the costs. 
VIII. PROCEEDINGS BEFORE THE COURT 
38.For a complete overview of the case, it should be recalled that, immediately after 
receiving the third-party application, the Guinean State submitted an application for 
review to the Court, which gave rise to the Court's judgment No. 
ECW/CCJ/JUD/49/23 of December 5, 2023. The court concluded that there were 
no new facts and found that Guinea had acted negligently in the main proceedings, 
declaring the application for review inadmissible. 
THE COURT'S ANALYSIS 
39. The Court recalls that third-party proceedings is an extraordinary remedy provided 
for in Article 91 ofits Rules of Procedure. The purpose of this procedure is to enable 
the Court to amend the contested judgment if it finds that it is prejudicial to the 
rights of the third-party opponent. 
40.Therefore, the Court is expected to determine whether the contested judgment is 
prejudicial to the rights of the third-party opponent and, if so, to indicate the changes 
to be made to its initial judgment. 
41.However, before reaching this stage, the Court must ensure that the conditions for 
admissibility set out in the aforementioned Article 91 are met. 
42.But first, we must ascertain the jurisdiction of the Court to hear the merits of the 
case. 
14 


IX. 
ON THE JURISDICTION 
43. The Court has jurisdiction to hear the instant case because it is based on a pre-
existing case over which this Court has previously established having jurisdiction .. 
This jurisdiction derives from the general authority conferred by Article 9 of the 
Supplementary Protocol(A/SP.1/01/05), in conjunction with Article 91 of the 
Court's Rules of Procedure, which specifically provides for the Court's power to 
review a dispute in third-party proceedings whenever a previous judgment could 
prejudice the rights of those who were not parties to the main proceedings. 
X. 
ON THE ADMISSIBILITY 
44. The aforementioned article states: 
Article 91: 
J. Articles 32 and 33 of these Rules shall apply to an application initiating 
third-party proceedings. In addition such an application shall: 
a) Specify the judgment contested; 
b) State how that judgment is prejudicial to the rights of the third-
party; 
c) Indicate the reasons for which the third-party was unable to take 
part in the original case. 
2. The application must be made against all the parties to the original 
case. 
3. Where the judgment has been published in the Official Journal of the 
community. The application must be lodged within two months of the 
publication. 
15 


4. The Court may, on application by the third-party, order a stay of 
execution of the judgment. 
5. The provisions of Title IIL Chapter L of these Rules shall apply. 
6. The contested judgment shall be varied on the points on which the 
submissions of the third-party are upheld. 
7. The original of the judgment in the third-party proceedings shall be 
annexed to the original of the contested judgment. A note of the 
judgment in the third-party proceedings shall be made in the margin 
of the original of the contested judgment. 
45 .It follows from these provisions that, in order to be accepted, a third-party proceeding 
must meet formal requirements and be filed within a certain time limit. 
46. Thus, before determining whether the judgment against which the third-party 
proceedings is filed is prejudicial to the rights of the third-party opponent, the Court 
must ensure that the third-party proceedings application is filed in the prescribed 
form and within the prescribed time limit. 
The conditions of formal admissibility 
4 7 .As far as the prescribed forms for the application are concerned, they are laid down 
in Article 91(1), (2) and (3) of the Rules of the Court. They are as follows: 
i) The provisions of Articles 32 and 33 of the Rules are applicable; 
ii) The contested judgment is specified; 
iii) It must be stated how the contested judgment is prejudicial to the rights 
of the opposing third-party; 
16 


iv) The reasons why the third-party opponent was unable to participate in 
the main proceedings must be stated. 
v) The application is brought against all the parties to the mam 
proceedings. 
48.Each of the above conditions will now be examined: 
• Compliance with the requirements of Articles 32 and 33 of the Rules of the 
Court 
49.The requirements of Articles 32 and 33 of the Rules of the Court must be checked 
by the Chief Registrar of the Court who, if necessary, invites the interested party to 
regularize its application. In the absence of an objection or deficiencies noted by the 
Court Chief Registrar as to whether the third-party proceedings complies with the 
requirements of Articles 32 and 33 of the Rules, the Court considers that it is not 
necessary to examine those requirements as regards the formal admissibility of the 
third-party proceedings. 
• Indication of the contested judgment 
50.ln the instant case, it is clear that the company Mobile Power Systems - FZE 
challenged Judgment No. ECW/CCJ/JUD/14/2020 of July 9, 2020, in Case 
ECW/CCJ/APP/19/18, Mr. Jbrahima Kassus Dioubate and K. Energie S.A. v. the 
Republic of Guinea. 
51. This condition is therefore met. 
• The reasons why the contested judgment is prejudicial to the rights of the 
third-party opponent 
52. The Third-party Opponent, Mobile Power Systems - FZE, asserts that K Energie 
S.A. never owned the mobile turbine power plants and associated facilities; that 
17 


Ibrahim Kassus Dioubate is fully aware of the fact that ownership of the power plant 
was not transferred to K Energie S.A. by Miami Capital Group Corporation or 
Mobile Power Systems - FZE; that K. Energie S.A. and Kassus Dioubate 
fraudulently concealed the identity of Mobile Power Systems - FZE, which is the 
true owner of the power plants, and claimed ownership before the Court with the 
intention of fraudulently distorting the facts and misleading the Court in order to 
obtain an advantage, which, incidentally, they obtained by the fact that the Court of 
Justice delivered a judgment in favor ofK Energie S.A. on July 9, 2020; that it was 
seriously prejudiced by the Court's judgment delivered on July 9, 2020, insofar as 
that judgment deprived it of its ownership and rights of possession of the mobile 
turbine installations and wrongfully conferred them on K Energie S.A. on the basis 
of a fraudulent statement. 
53.In the contested judgment, the Court found that the State Guinea had violated K. 
ENERGIE SA, consisting of power stations with mobile turbines, associated 
facilities, in particular transfonners and other equipment necessary for the production 
and distribution of electricity; buildings and real estate constructed to house offices 
and residences for employees (see operative clause of the judgment). 
54.In the instant case, Mobile Power Systems - FZE claims that the turbines are not 
owned by K. Energie S.A. Energie S.A. Clearly, if the third-party opponent can prove 
that it is the owner of the said assets or that K. Energie is not the true owner, it will 
have proved that the contested judgment is prejudicial to its right. 
55.Consequently, Mobile Power Systems- FZE indicated the reasons why the contested 
judgment is prejudicial to it and justified the admissibility of its application to that 
effect. 
• Indication of the reasons why the third-party opponent could not 
participate in the main proceedings 
18 


56.The Third-Party Opponent, Mobile Power Systems-FZE, claims that it was unable 
to participate in the proceedings because it only became aware of them when the 
judgment was published by Ibrahim Kassus Dioubate on the K Energie S.A. website; 
that the Applicant's right to a fair hearing was violated because it was denied the 
opportunity to be heard in proceedings concerning the ownership of mobile turbine 
power stations, which gave rise to a judgment by the Court. 
57.It is recalled that on April 23, 2018, the date on which the initiating application was 
submitted to the Court of Justice, Mr. Alexandre Sam Zormati (sole shareholder of 
Mobile Power Systems - FZE) had been removed from his position as general 
manager ofK Energie SA, in which he held 1%, on January 25, 2018, and replaced 
by Mr. Kassus Dioubate, previously deputy general manager. The two partners were 
already at odds. There were multiple legal proceedings before the Guinean and 
American courts. It is perfectly conceivable that Alexandre Sam Zormati and, 
consequently, Mobile Power Systems - FZE, were unaware of the initial process. 
This condition has therefore been met. 
• The application must be made against all the parties to the original case. 
58.The parties to the main proceedings are Mr. Ibrahima Kassus Dioubate, K. Energie 
S.A. and the State of Guinea. The third-party proceeding is brought against all these 
parties. Therefore, this condition is also met. 
59.In short, Mobile Power Systems - FZE's third-party proceedings application meets 
the criteria set out in Articles 32 and 33 of the Court's Rules of Procedure, specifies 
the contested judgment, indicates how that judgment affects its interests, states the 
reasons why it was unable to participate in the main proceedings and, finally, it is 
brought against all the parties to the main proceedings, which are Kassus Dioubate, 
K. Energie S.A. and the State of Guinea. 
19 


60.From the above, it can be said that the third-party proceedings application meets the 
formal admissibility conditions. The admissibility condition relating to the deadline 
must now be assessed. 
The deadline condition 
61. With regard to the time limit, Article 91 (3) of the Rules provides for two months, 
starting, in principle, from the date of publication of the judgment in the Official 
Journal of the Community, if such publication has been made. In fact, the wording 
of paragraph 3 clearly indicates that the application of the time limit condition is 
hypothetical, since, in order to be applicable, this condition is subject to the 
publication of the judgment in the Official Journal of the Community. 
62.Furthermore, the Court, in interpreting Article 91(3), has already stated, in Judgment 
No. ECW/CCJ/JUD/02/21 of February 25, 2021 in the third-party proceedings 
Pastor Ekpemsidem Dan Ekpemsidem & 6.499 Ors v. Vincent Agu and 19 Ors, 
Federal Republic of Nigeria and 5 Ors, that: 
"The prerequisite for the application of this rule is the publication of the 
judgment in the Official Journal of the Community, after which the time-
limit begins to run from the date on which it was published(..)" (§65). 
63.However, it should be noted that the contested judgment was not published in the 
Official Journal of the Community. Under these conditions, no deadline can be 
calculated from that date. Therefore, in the absence of publication of the judgment 
in the Official Journal of the Community, Article 91(3) of the Regulation should not 
apply. 
64.ln any event, the Court notes that the third-party opponent filed its application on 
September 18, 2020, that is, 2 months and 10 days after the delivery of the contested 
20 


judgment, which satisfies the requirement of a reasonable time provided for in the 
aforementioned provision. 
65 .It can therefore be said that the third-party proceedings application meets the 
admissibility conditions relating to the time limit. 
66.Accordingly, Mobile Power Systems - FZE's third-party proceedings application 
must be declared admissible. 
XI. 
ON THE MERIT 
67. The Court must now examine whether the instant third-party proceedings application 
makes it possible to demonstrate, taking into account the arguments of all the parties, 
that the judgment of July 9, 2020 is prejudicial to the rights of Mobile Power Systems 
- FZE. If the Court concludes that the judgment is prejudicial to the rights of the 
third-party opponent, it must then indicate to what extent that conclusion is likely to 
alter the judgment of July 9, 2020 and in what direction that judgment should be 
amended. 
68.Mobile Power Systems - FZE claims ownership of the turbines, associated facilities 
and auxiliary components. The contested judgment recognized K Energie S.A. as the 
owner of these assets. This is because the Court recognized K. Energie S.A.'s 
exclusive ownership of the assets in question and declared that the State of Guinea 
had violated its right of ownership by excluding it from the facilities and replacing it 
with another company. However, if it is proven that K Energie SA is not the owner 
of said property or that ownership is still in dispute between Mobile Power Systems 
- FZE and K Energie SA, private parties, then the judgment of July 9, 2020 would 
have erroneously attributed ownership of said property to K Energie SA or else the 
Court, in deciding as it did, would have ventured onto ground that is not its own. In 
fact, the role of the Court, when an action for the protection of property rights is 
21 


brought before it, is certainly not to grant title to the property in question, but to use 
the existing titles as a basis for determining whether or not the property right has 
been violated. If the Court comes to such a conclusion, it must therefore conclude 
that the judgment of July 9, 2020 is prejudicial to the rights of Mobile Power Systems 
- FZE or that it has examined a claim whose merits are a dispute between private 
parties and which therefore does not fall under the protection of human rights. 
69. Thus, if the facts and circumstances resulting from the initial proceedings and the 
third-party proceedings reveal that Mobile Power Systems - FZE is the beneficial 
owner of the turbines, associated facilities and ancillary components, or that K. 
Energie S.A. does not have ownership of the assets, or that ownership of the assets 
is disputed between Mobile Power Systems - FZE and K Energie S.A., private 
parties, then the Court would have to amend its judgment of July 9, 2020. 
Does the third-party proceedings application make it possible to establish that the 
judgment of July 9, 2020 is prejudicial to the rights of Mobile Power Systems -
FZE? 
70.It follows from the above facts that: 
i) 
K. Energie S.A. was registered in the Registre du Commerce et du 
Credit Mobilier (Commercial and Mobile Credit Register - RCCM) on 
24 May 2014, in accordance with its amended articles of association 
[amendment made following the resignation on 25 January 2018 of 
Sam Alexandre Zormati as Chief Executive Officer and the 
appointment of Mr Kassus Dioubate as his substitute; the latter being 
the former Deputy General Manager. The amendment to the articles of 
association only concerns the appointment of a new general manager] 
dated January 25, 2018 [document contained in the application 
22 


submitted to the Court by K. Energie S.A. on April 23, 2018], 93% of 
the capital is owned by Miami Capital Holding Corporation, 5% by 
Miami Group Inc (presumably Miami Capital Group Corporation), and 
1 % by Mr. Kassus Ibrahima Dioubate and 1 % by Mr. Sam Alexandre 
Zormati; 
ii) 
The electricity supply contract between K Energie S.A. and the State 
of Guinea is dated August 1, 2014; 
iii) 
In its third-party proceedings, K Energie S.A. indicated that Miami 
Capital Group Holding is jointly held by Ibrahim Kassus Dioubate 
(49%) and Sam Alexandre Zormati (49%). 
iv) 
With regard to the articles of association ofK. Energie S.A., one might 
think that Miami Capital Group Holding and Miami Capital Group Inc. 
(Miami Capital Group Corporation) were created before K. Energie 
S.A., since they are all shareholders of the latter; however, the deed of 
incorporation of Miami Capital Group Corporation indicates that it was 
registered on 22 January 2016, in the Dominican Republic; 
furthermore, nowhere in the documents submitted to the Court 
(initiating application, third-party proceedings application, request for 
review) is there any trace of the articles of association or the date of 
incorporation of Miami Capital Group Holding, except for the 
statements regarding the composition of its capital made by K. Energie 
S.A. and referred to in the preceding paragraph; 
v) 
The equipment lease agreement between Miami Capital Group 
Corporation, on the one hand, and Miami Capital Group Holding and 
K. Energie S.A., on the other hand, was signed on January 5, 2015; the 
lease period runs from 5 January 2015 to 5 March 2017; 
23 


vi) 
The contract for the purchase of the turbines was signed on January 12, 
2014, between Miami Capital Group Holding and PW Power Systems 
Inc. prior to the lease agreement; 
vii) 
It was due to the UBA' s requirements for establishing the credit line, 
namely that a ''power plant sales contract in the name of a third-party 
was not sufficient, even if that third-party was a member of the group 
that signed the energy supply contract with the State of Guinea", that 
the shareholders of Miami Capital Group Holding decided to propose 
K. Energie S.A., which had signed the energy sales contract with the 
government, to benefit from the credit line; K. Energie S.A. thus 
established a relationship with PW Power System Inc. for operational 
reasons. These elements provided by K Energie SA confirm, at the very 
least, that the contract for the purchase of the power plants was actually 
concluded between PW Power System Inc. and K Energie SA; 
viii) There was a case in Guinea in which Alexandre Sam Zormati was 
prosecuted by the Public Prosecutor's Office, with Ibrahim Kassus 
Dioubate and K Energie S.A. as civil parties. By judgment no. 129 of 
November 22, 2017 of the Second Correctional Chamber of the Court 
of Appeal of Conakry, he was convicted of abuse of social assets, 
complicity by instigation in the falsification of private documents and 
use of false documents, fraud, complicity by instigation in the 
falsification of authentic documents, sentenced to 5 years in prison and 
an arrest warrant was issued. It was also ordered to pay Kassus 
Dioubate and K Energie S.A. the sum of 500,000,000 US dollars for all 
damages suffered; 
24 


ix) 
There is also another case before the Court of First Instance ofKaloum-
Conakry between K Energie SA and Societt.~ guineenne de l'Energie 
SA. The First Civil and Administrative Chamber delivered Judgment 
No. 189 of December 28, 2017, in which it ruled that GDE Sarlu be 
expelled from the lands of the maritime port domain of Coronthie. GDE 
Sarlu appealed against this judgment; 
x) 
On September 19, 2019, the Kaloum Court of First Instance, ruling in 
civil matters, delivered judgment no. 148, according to which it 
"ordered the return of the said power plants and associated facilities 
owned by the Guinean electricity company (GDE) Sarlu to the company 
K Energie" and "acquitted the State of Guinea". This judgment was 
delivered after the case was referred to the Court on April 23, 2018 by 
Kassus Dioubate and K Energie S.A. and before the judgment of July 
9, 2020. None of the parties drew the Court's attention to this 
information. K Energie S.A. indicates, in the pleading submitted to the 
Court on November 13, 2020, without further clarification, that this 
process has not yet been concluded; 
xi) 
Another case, before the USA courts, concerns the dispute over the 
ownership of the assets in question. In this context, the United States 
District Court for the District of Maryland issued an order on March 
13, 2017 authorizing the handover of the returned turbine to Miami 
Capital Group Corp., perpetually enjoining K Energie SA and Ibrahim 
Kassus Dioubate from interfering with the property or possession of 
Miami Capital Group Corp. 
71.Contrary to what K. Energie S.A. claims, the Court did not consider, in paragraphs 
285,296 to 301 of its judgment, the argument that the turbines are owned by Mobile 
25 


Power Systems - FZE. Instead, it examined the argument that PW Power Systems 
Inc., the supplier of the turbines, continued to own them. 
72.From this point of view, the argument that Mobile Power Systems-FZE owns the 
turbines, as a result of the transfers of ownership made by Miami Capital Group 
Corporation is anew argument, supported by new facts: the lease of the turbines, the 
title deeds and the decision of the Maryland Court of Justice. 
73.It is clear that, despite court rulings in Guinea and the United States, the dispute over 
the ownership of mobile turbines, their parts and accessories is still ongoing: K. 
Energie S.A., Miami Capital Group Corporation, Mobile Power Systems - FZE, is 
still in progress. 
7 4.In this regard, the third-party proceedings revealed two other new facts that the Court 
was unaware of at the time of its decision: 
a. the existence of a turbine purchase agreement signed on January 12, 2014, 
between PW Power System and Miami Capital Group Holding (see 69.vi); 
b. that judgment No. 148 of the Kaloum Court of First Instance in civil 
matters, delivered on September 19, 2019, acquitted the State of Guinean 
and ordered the devolution of the power plants to K Energie SA (see 69.x). 
75.It also clarifies the reasons why, although Miami Capital Group Holding entered into 
a contract with PW Power System Inc. to purchase the turbines, it was K Energie 
SA, 93% owned by Miami Capital Group Holding, that made the payments (see 
69.vii). 
76.All these elements support the idea that the case brought before the Court by Ibrahim 
Kassus Dioubate and K Energie S.A., which gave rise to the judgment of July 9, 
26 


2020, is predominantly a contractual matter in its civil and criminal aspects, 
including the criminal liability of company directors. 
77.It is clear that at no time did the State of Guinea transfer ownership of the turbines 
to any entity or individual. On the contrary, it contracted in good faith, initially, on 
August 1, 2014, with K. Energie S.A., whose legal representative until January 25, 
2018 was Mr. Alexandre Zormati, its Managing Director. The latter was not only the 
administrative director ofK Energie S.A.; he also was the director of Miami Capital 
Group Corporation and Miami Capital Group Holding at the time the State of Guinea 
entered into a contract with GOU Sarlu on 12 January 2017. 
78.lt is clear that the dispute between the partners Kassus Dioubate and Alexandre 
Zormati is the real cause of the series of events that followed the termination of the 
electricity purchase contract between the State of Guinea and K Energie S.A. This 
dispute between the partners was a private dispute that was the subject of several 
legal proceedings still pending in the Guinean courts at the time the main case was 
submitted to this Court. 
79.All the factual circumstances, as they appear from the initiating application and the 
third-party proceedings application, indicate that the claim brought against the State 
of Guinea in the main proceedings essentially concerned a private dispute between 
shareholders of various companies, a dispute which ended up having a negative 
impact on the supply of electricity to Conakry and led the State of Guinea to seek 
other partnerships. 
80.From the above, the Court is entitled to consider that the third-party Opponent has 
succeeded in establishing before it the serious legal consequences that the judgment 
of July 9, 2020 is likely to have on its rights and property. The third-party 
proceedings also made it possible to put the dispute back into its true context, which 
is that of an essentially private dispute between partners and whose persistence led 
27 


to the intervention of the State of Guinea to guarantee the supply of electricity to the 
population of the capital Conakry. 
81.Consequently, the Third-Party Opponent has established that the judgment of July 9, 
2020, is prejudicial to its rights. 
In what way should the judgment of July 9, 2020 he amended? 
82.Mobile Power Systems - FZE, Third-Party Opponent, asks the Court to annul the 
judgment of July 9, 2020 for lack of jurisdiction, fraudulent statements and failure to 
constitute the real litigants. 
83 .It follows from all the new facts and circumstances relating to the case, as indicated 
in the preceding paragraphs, that the Court, ab initio, was called upon to hear a case 
that it would have immediately identified as a private dispute between commercial 
partners, had it not been for the fragmentary information provided by the parties in 
the initiating application; in reality, it was not a case of human rights violation. 
84.Moreover, when the Court delivered its judgment of July 9, 2020, several lawsuits 
brought by Kassus Dioubate and K Energie against Alexandre Zormati, GDE Sarlu 
and the State of Guinean were pending before the Guinean courts. Several decisions 
were handed down, as set out in the conclusions presented to the Court: Judgment 
No. 469 of 21 November 2016; order for partial dismissal and referral to the criminal 
court of 11 September 2017, handed down by the investigating judge of the second 
investigating chamber; Judgment No. 24 of 22 November 2017 of the Second 
Investigation Control Section of the Conakry Court of Appeal; judgment No. 129 of 
22 November 2017 of the Second Criminal Division of the Conakry Court of Appeal, 
overruling judgment No. 118 of 12 September 2017 of the Kaloum Court of First 
Instance (fraud, forgery and use of false public and private documents); interlocutory 
decision of 20 December 2017 suspending the proceedings of the Kaloum Court of 
28 


First Instance; judgment of the Criminal Court No. 157 of 20 December 2017 of the 
Kaloum Court of First Instance; judgment No. 189 of28 December 2017 of the first 
civil and administrative chamber of the Kaloum Court ofFirst Instance (ordering the 
expulsion of GDU Sarlu from the Coronthie port maritime domain legally assigned 
to K. A Energie SA, but refusing to comply with the judgment through provisional 
enforcement; GDE Sarlu wish to lodge an appeal against the decision); Judgment 
No. 148 of 19 September 2019 of the Conakry Court of First Instance. 
85. The purpose of all these proceedings is to recover possession of the port area 
allocated to K Energie S.A. for the installation of the power plants and to recognize 
K Energie S.A.'s right of ownership over the power plants, as well as to establish the 
fraud, forgery and use of false documents by Alexandre Sam Zormati to deprive K 
Energie S.A. of the said power plants. As a result, there was and still is a high risk 
that the Court, when examining the case, would call into question the decisions of 
the Guinean courts or set itself up as a court of appeal, with a high risk of conflicting 
decisions. 
86.From the above, the Court observes that, at the time the initiating application was 
filed on April 23, 2018, the ownership of the assets in question was contested 
between K Energie S.A. and Mobile Power Systems - FZE. In fact, it was not up to 
the Court to decide, in the context of a dispute concerning the protection of property 
rights, a private dispute between commercial partners concerning the ownership of 
goods in dispute and in the absence of one of the main protagonists. Disputes relating 
to the ownership of property between private individuals fall, in principle, under the 
jurisdiction of national courts. However, the instant case has clearly demonstrated 
that K Energie S.A. wrongly claimed and was recognized as the owner of the turbines 
and their accessories. 
29 


87. The Court finds that, insofar as the judgment of 9 July 2020 was based essentially on 
the presumption that K Energie was the owner, a presumption based on the latter's 
allegations, which the Respondent State was then unable to challenge, but which the 
present third-party proceedings have completely called into question, it is appropriate 
to strike out from the Court's judgment any element that could affect the rights and 
interests of the Third-Party Opponent. 
88.In this regard, it is clear that, by leaving unchanged in its judgment of 9 July 2020, 
the conclusion that the turbines and auxiliary components belong to K Energie S.A., 
that judgment is likely to affect the rights of Mobile Power Systems - FZE. 
Accordingly, the analyses and conclusions concerning the violation of the right to 
property, wrongly attributed to K Energie S.A. should be struck out from the said 
judgment, in particular paragraphs 205 to 306 of that judgment. As a necessary 
consequence, the finding of infringement of K Energie S.A. 's right to property by 
the State of Guinea and the reparations ordered in its favor in the operative clause of 
the contested judgment are no longer justified and must therefore be amended. 
XII. 
ON COSTS: 
89 .In view of the circumstances of the instant case, it must be declared that each party 
shall bear its own costs, in accordance with Article 66 of the Rules of Court. 
XIII. 
OPERATIVE CLAUSE 
90. For these reasons, the Court held a public hearing and having heard the parties: 
On the Jurisdiction: 
1. 
The Court declares itself competent; 
On the Admissibility: 
30 


n. 
Declares admissible Mobile Power Systems -
FZE's third-party 
proceedings application; 
The merits of the case: 
111. 
Declares that the Third-Party Opponent has proved that the 
ECW/CCJ/UD/14/20 judgment of July 9, 2020 violates its rights; 
1v. 
Declares that the third-party proceedings application has sufficiently 
proven that K Energie S.A. wrongfully claimed ownership of the power 
stations and caused them to be recognized as its property; 
v. 
Declares that paragraphs 205 to 306 have been struck out from the 
judgment of July 9, 2020 in the case of Ibrahima Kassus Dioubate and K 
Energie v. State of Guinea (No. ECW/CCJ/JUD/14/20); 
v1. 
Declares that paragraphs 381 and 384, relating to the recognition of K 
Energie S.A.'s right to property and the award of reparations, respectively, 
have been struck out from the operative clause of that judgment; 
vii. 
Orders that the original of this judgment be annexed to the original 
judgment of July 9, 2020 (No. ECW/CCJ/UD/14/20), in the margins of 
which reference will be made to this judgment; 
ONCOSTS: 
91.Declares that each party shall bear its own costs. 
31
